proposition: The Ohio Supreme Court held that actual physical control requires a person to be in the driver's seat, behind the steering wheel, in possession of the ignition key, and physically capable of starting the engine. The statutory definition in R.C. 4511.194(A)(2) is a near-verbatim codification of two of the four Kelley factors.
quoted_text: ["actual physical control . . . requires that a person be in the driver's seat of a vehicle, behind the steering wheel, in possession of the ignition key, and in such condition that he is physically capable of starting the engine and causing the vehicle to move"]
quote_check: [{"quote": "actual physical control . . . requires that a person be in the driver's seat of a vehicle, behind the steering wheel, in possession of the ignition key, and in such condition that he is physically capable of starting the engine and causing the vehicle to move", "result": "CLOSE", "similarity": 0.77, "matched_passage": "hicles by persons whose faculties are appreciably impaired from the consumption of alcohol or use of drugs of abuse. \" : Therefore, the term \u201cactual physical control,\u201d as employed in the subject ordinance, requires that a person be in the driver\u2019s se\u00e1t of a vehicle, behind the'steering wheel, *98 in possession'of the ignition.key, and.in snch condition that hie is physically capable of starting the engine and causing-the vehicle to move. 4 Under this test, the instant record supports a conclusion that appellee was under the influence of alcoh"}]
brief_block: Cincinnati v. Kelley, 47 Ohio St.2d 94 (1976). Memo: "The Ohio Supreme Court held that actual physical control requires a person to be in the driver's seat, behind the steering wheel, in possession of the ignition key, and physically capable of starting the engine. The statutory definition in R.C. 4511.194(A)(2) is a near-verbatim codification of two of the four Kelley factors." Quoted text: "actual physical control . . . requires that a person be in the driver's seat of a vehicle, behind the steering wheel, in possession of the ignition key, and in such condition that he is physically capable of starting the engine and causing the vehicle to move."
opinion_block: Therefore, the term "actual physical control," as employed in the subject ordinance, requires that a person be in the driver's seat of a vehicle, behind the steering wheel, in possession of the ignition key, and in such condition that he is physically capable of starting the engine and causing the vehicle to move.
finding_analysis: The proposition is directly supported by the Kelley opinion. The CourtListener OCR of the source page contains characteristic artifacts ("driver's seÃ¡t," "behind the'steering wheel," "ignition.key," "snch condition," "hie is physically capable," "causing-the vehicle"), which fully account for the ~0.77 similarity drag. After silently correcting those OCR errors, the opinion's holding language is verbatim identical to the memo's quote. The memo's gloss that R.C. 4511.194(A)(2) codifies two of Kelley's four factors (driver's position and possession of the ignition key, but not steering-wheel posture or physical capability to start the engine) is a fair characterization of the statutory text quoted in the memo.

quoted_text: ["a person in the driver's position in the front seat with the ignition key in his possession indicating either his actual or potential movement of the vehicle while under the influence of alcohol or any drug of abuse can be found in violation of R.C. 4511.19(A)(1)"]

Operation of a motor vehicle within contemplation of the statute is a broader term than mere driving and a person in the driver\u2019s position in the front seat with the ignition key in his possession indicating either his actual or potential movement of the vehicle while under the influence of alcohol or any drug of abuse can be found in violation of R.C. 4511.19(A)(1). 1 The question before this court is whether the conduct described in the instant case"}]
brief_block: State v. Cleary, 22 Ohio St.3d 198 (1986). Memo: "Pre-2004 Supreme Court broadening of 'operation' under former R.C. 4511.19 â€” a person in the driver's position in the front seat with the ignition key in his possession indicating either actual or potential movement of the vehicle while under the influence can be found in violation." Quoted text: "a person in the driver's position in the front seat with the ignition key in his possession indicating either his actual or potential movement of the vehicle while under the influence of alcohol or any drug of abuse can be found in violation of R.C. 4511.19(A)(1)."
opinion_block: Operation of a motor vehicle within contemplation of the statute is a broader term than mere driving and a person in the driver's position in the front seat with the ignition key in his possession indicating either his actual or potential movement of the vehicle while under the influence of alcohol or any drug of abuse can be found in violation of R.C. 4511.19(A)(1).
finding_analysis: The quoted language appears verbatim at page 199 of the Cleary opinion. The ~0.80 similarity score is a function of CourtListener OCR artifacts in the surrounding text rather than any divergence in the substance of the quote. The memo's proposition â€” that Cleary represents the pre-2004 Supreme Court's broadening of "operation" under former R.C. 4511.19 â€” is well supported: Cleary expressly held that "operating" is broader than "driving" and reached a defendant found asleep in the driver's seat of a parked car with the motor running, affirming conviction on a strict-liability rationale.

quoted_text: ["[a] clear purpose of R.C. 4511.19 is to discourage persons from putting themselves in the position in which they can potentially cause the movement of a motor vehicle while intoxicated . . . . Prohibition of potentially harmful conduct need not await occurrence of the act."]

Prohibition of potentially harmful conduct need not await occurrence of the act.", "result": "CLOSE", "similarity": 0.79, "matched_passage": "vehicle\u2019s engine after consuming alcohol or that the engine was running at the time the defendant is apprehended. A clear purpose of R.C. 4511.19 is to discourage persons from putting themselves in the position in which they can potentially cause the movement of a motor vehicle while intoxicated or under the influence of any drug of abuse. Accord Cleary and McGlone, supra. Prohibition of potentially harmful conduct need not await occurrence of the act. As we, in effect, held in Cleary , the intent of the legislature in enacting strong legislation involving"}]
brief_block: State v. Gill, 70 Ohio St.3d 150 (1994). Memo: "Clarifies that the statutory purpose is to discourage persons from putting themselves in a position to potentially cause vehicle movement while intoxicated; prohibition of potentially harmful conduct need not await occurrence of the act." Quoted text: "[a] clear purpose of R.C. 4511.19 is to discourage persons from putting themselves in the position in which they can potentially cause the movement of a motor vehicle while intoxicated . . . . Prohibition of potentially harmful conduct need not await occurrence of the act."
opinion_block: A clear purpose of R.C. 4511.19 is to discourage persons from putting themselves in the position in which they can potentially cause the movement of a motor vehicle while intoxicated or under the influence of any drug of abuse. ... Prohibition of potentially harmful conduct need not await occurrence of the act.
finding_analysis: Both quoted sentences appear at page 154 of the Gill opinion, in consecutive sentences within the same paragraph. The memo's ellipsis silently elides only the phrase "or under the influence of any drug of abuse" and the closing of the first sentence, neither of which alters the meaning. The ~0.79 similarity drag is OCR-noise in the CourtListener text, not paraphrase. The memo's proposition â€” that Gill clarifies the statutory purpose as discouraging position-of-potential-movement and rejects the need to await the harmful act â€” is precisely Gill's reasoning and is the basis on which the Court extended Cleary/McGlone to defendants whose engines were not running.

proposition: Holds that driver's seat plus key in ignition sufficed for operation under former R.C. 4511.19; part of the pre-2004 doctrinal expansion.
brief_block: State v. McGlone, 59 Ohio St.3d 122 (1991). Memo: "Holds that driver's seat plus key in ignition sufficed for operation under former R.C. 4511.19; part of the pre-2004 doctrinal expansion."
opinion_block: We hold that an intoxicated person who is in the driver's seat of a motor vehicle parked on private or public property with the key in the ignition is operating the vehicle in violation of R.C. 4511.19(A)(1).
finding_analysis: No direct quote was attributed to McGlone in the memo, so there is no quote-accuracy issue. The memo's substantive proposition is directly supported: McGlone's syllabus and concluding paragraph hold that an intoxicated person in the driver's seat of a parked vehicle with the key in the ignition is "operating" the vehicle in violation of former R.C. 4511.19(A)(1). The memo's characterization of McGlone as part of the pre-2004 doctrinal expansion of "operation" is consistent with both McGlone's own reliance on Cleary and the later Gill court's treatment of McGlone as part of that progression.

proposition: Traces S.B. 123 legislative history; explains the post-2004 split between OVI (operation/movement) and physical control (no movement).
cited_case: State v. Wallace, 166 Ohio App.3d 845, 2006-Ohio-2477 (1st Dist.)
brief_block: State v. Wallace, 166 Ohio App.3d 845, 2006-Ohio-2477 (1st Dist.). Memo: "Traces S.B. 123 legislative history; explains the post-2004 split between OVI (operation/movement) and physical control (no movement)."
finding_analysis: No direct quote was attributed to Wallace in the memo, so there is no quote-accuracy issue. The memo's proposition is directly supported by the body of the opinion, particularly paragraphs 8 through 14. Wallace expressly recites that until Sub.S.B. No. 123 took effect on January 1, 2004, the meaning of "operate" had been exclusively a matter of judicial interpretation; it walks through Cleary, McGlone, and Gill; it identifies the statutory redefinition of "operate" in R.C. 4511.01(HHH) as "to cause or have caused movement of a vehicle"; and it explicitly notes that "the General Assembly also created in R.C. 4511.194 the new offense of having physical control of a vehicle while under the influence" to address the Gill-type no-movement scenarios. That is exactly the legislative-history-and-post-2004-split account the memo attributes to it.

proposition: Pfeifer J. dissent in Gill described the pre-2004 doctrinal expansion as motivated by concern about drunk radio listeners or people who use their cars as a four-wheeled heated hotel room.
quoted_text: ["drunk radio listeners, or people who use their cars as a four-wheeled, heated hotel room"]
quote_check: [{"quote": "drunk radio listeners, or people who use their cars as a four-wheeled, heated hotel room", "result": "VERBATIM", "similarity": 1.0, "matched_passage": "modification narrows the definition of \"operate,\" which effectively eliminates \"drunk radio listeners, or people who use their cars as a four-wheeled, heated hotel room\" from being convicted of OVI."}]
brief_block: [I]n a series the Court of Appeals later described as motivated by concern about "drunk radio listeners, or people who use their cars as a four-wheeled, heated hotel room." State v. Schultz, 2008-Ohio-4448, Â¶ 19 (8th Dist.) (quoting Pfeifer, J., dissenting in State v. Gill).
opinion_block: This modification narrows the definition of "operate," which effectively eliminates "drunk radio listeners, or people who use their cars as a four-wheeled, heated hotel room" from being convicted of OVI. Gill, 70 Ohio St.3d at 157-158 (Pfeifer, J., dissenting). [Schultz Â¶ 19, with footnote 2 reproducing the longer Pfeifer dissent: "The legislature's intent was to deter and to punish drunk drivers, not to punish drunk radio listeners, or people who use their cars as a four-wheeled, heated hotel room."]
finding_analysis: The quoted phrase is verbatim and is correctly attributed to Pfeifer, J., dissenting in Gill (Schultz Â¶ 19 quotes the dissent directly, and footnote 2 of Schultz reproduces the surrounding passage in full). The memo's framing is essentially accurate: Pfeifer's dissent criticized the Cleary/McGlone/Gill line of expansion and argued the legislature's intent was to deter drunk drivers, not drunk radio listeners. The memo characterizes that expansion line as the target of Pfeifer's criticism, which is fair. One subtle point: Schultz uses the quote to describe what the 2004 narrowing of "operate" eliminates from OVI, not directly to say the expansion was "motivated by" that concern â€” but the underlying Pfeifer dissent does function as a critique of the prior expansion's reach, so the proposition fairly captures the citation's role. Supported.

proposition: Schultz footnote 6 holds that an impaired passenger cannot be convicted of a physical control violation under R.C. 4511.194 because he is not in the driver's seat.
quoted_text: ["the term 'operate' is still broad enough to encompass an impaired passenger who grabs the steering wheel of a moving vehicle. . . . But an impaired passenger cannot be convicted of a physical control violation because he is not in the driver's seat. See R.C. 4511.194(A)(2)"]
quote_check: [{"quote": "the term 'operate' is still broad enough to encompass an impaired passenger who grabs the steering wheel of a moving vehicle. . . . But an impaired passenger cannot be convicted of a physical control violation because he is not in the driver's seat. See R.C. 4511.194(A)(2)", "result": "CLOSE", "similarity": 0.79, "matched_passage": "ordinance' and `municipal OVI offense' mean any municipal ordinance prohibiting a person from operating a vehicle while under the influence of alcohol, a drug of abuse, * * *.\" 6 The term \"operate\" is still broad enough to encompass an impaired passenger who grabs the steering wheel of a moving vehicle. See Wallace , supra. But an impaired passenger cannot be convicted of a physical control violation because he is not in the driver's seat. See R.C. 4511.194 (A)(2). 7 \"Equivalent offenses\" include involuntary manslaughter with an impaired driving finding under R."}]
brief_block: Schultz observed in footnote 6 that "the term 'operate' is still broad enough to encompass an impaired passenger who grabs the steering wheel of a moving vehicle. . . . But an impaired passenger cannot be convicted of a physical control violation because he is not in the driver's seat. See R.C. 4511.194(A)(2)." Id. fn. 6 (emphasis added).
opinion_block: The term "operate" is still broad enough to encompass an impaired passenger who grabs the steering wheel of a moving vehicle. See Wallace, supra. But an impaired passenger cannot be convicted of a physical control violation because he is not in the driver's seat. See R.C. 4511.194(A)(2). [Schultz footnote 6.]
finding_analysis: The quote in the memo is essentially verbatim against footnote 6 of Schultz. The memo's ellipsis (". . .") elides only the citation signal "See Wallace, supra." between the two sentences â€” a fair editorial omission that does not alter meaning. The 0.79 similarity flag is attributable to that omitted signal plus CL OCR noise; the substance is verbatim. The proposition (Schultz fn 6 says an impaired passenger cannot be convicted of physical control because not in the driver's seat) is directly stated by the footnote. Note that fn 6 is dictum (Schultz's holding is about whether physical control is an "equivalent offense" for OVI enhancement), but the memo accurately characterizes it as a footnote observation, not as a holding of the case's primary issue. Supported.

proposition: Probable cause for physical control / OVI is not vitiated by keys being out of the ignition with the vehicle legally parked and the motor off.
quoted_text: ["probable cause is not vitiated by the fact that appellant was legally parked, with the motor turned off, and the keys not in the ignition."]

brief_block: The Eighth District held there was probable cause to arrest him for both OVI and physical control, and stated: "probable cause is not vitiated by the fact that appellant was legally parked, with the motor turned off, and the keys not in the ignition." Id. Â¶ 23.
opinion_block: Given these facts, the police had probable cause to arrest appellant on the scene. Further, based on the previously mentioned analysis, probable cause is not vitiated by the fact that appellant was legally parked, with the motor turned off, and the keys not in the ignition. [Zingale Â¶ 23.]
finding_analysis: The quoted language is verbatim against Zingale Â¶ 23. The proposition is directly supported. The facts in Zingale match the memo's framing: defendant was found lying across the driver's seat into the passenger's seat with the keys in his right hand, vehicle parked, motor off, keys not in ignition; the court affirmed probable cause for both OVI and the physical control charge. Supported.

proposition: Keys retrieved by officer from the back seat in plain view were 'within her reach from the driver's seat'; the court held the defendant had possession of the ignition key.
cited_case: State v. Vanderkooi, 82 Ohio App.3d 173 (3d Dist. 1992)
quoted_text: ["within her reach from the driver's seat", "she had possession of the ignition key"]
quote_check: [{"quote": "within her reach from the driver's seat", "result": "CLOSE", "similarity": 0.78, "matched_passage": "4511.19(A)(1). As appellant\u2019s keys were within her reach from the driver\u2019s seat, she had possession of the ignition key."}, {"quote": "she had possession of the ignition key", "result": "VERBATIM", "similarity": 1.0, "matched_passage": "As appellant\u2019s keys were within her reach from the driver\u2019s seat, she had possession of the ignition key. Therefore, there was a potential for movement of the appellant\u2019s vehicle."}]
brief_block: In State v. Vanderkooi, 82 Ohio App.3d 173 (3d Dist. 1992), the keys were retrieved by the officer from the back seat in plain view; the court held they were "within her reach from the driver's seat" and that "she had possession of the ignition key." Id. at 175.
opinion_block: As appellant's keys were within her reach from the driver's seat, she had possession of the ignition key. Therefore, there was a potential for movement of the appellant's vehicle. [Vanderkooi at 175.] . . . Herein, the patrolman testified that he retrieved appellant's keys from plain view in the back seat.
finding_analysis: Both quoted snippets are verbatim against the opinion. The factual framing is accurate: Jimmie threw the keys to appellant in the back seat; the patrolman observed them in plain view in the back seat and retrieved them; the court at p. 175 held that because the keys were "within her reach from the driver's seat," she had "possession of the ignition key." The 0.78 similarity flag reflects CL OCR/whitespace noise rather than any substantive divergence; the substance is verbatim. Supported.

proposition: Driver's seat plus key in possession (not in ignition) sufficient for 'operation' under former R.C. 4511.19.
cited_case: State v. Barger, 78 Ohio App.3d 451 (5th Dist. 1992)
brief_block: State v. Barger, 78 Ohio App.3d 451 (5th Dist. 1992) . . . reached the same conclusion where the defendant was in the driver's seat with the key in her possession but not in the ignition.
opinion_block: In construing the evidence in the light most favorable to the prosecution, the evidence shows that (1) the headlights were on, (2) defendant was leaning out the driver's side of the truck and, then, sat up in the driver's seat, (3) the motor was not running and (4) the keys were not in the ignition, but defendant produced the keys from her person when later requested. . . . [T]he defendant had the ignition key in her possession, which indicated her "potential movement of the vehicle." Based upon the foregoing evidence, we find that there was sufficient evidence upon which a rational trier of fact could find that the defendant operated the truck.
finding_analysis: No direct quotes in the memo for this row, so no quote check is needed. The proposition is directly supported: Barger affirmed an R.C. 4511.19(A)(1) conviction where (a) defendant was in the driver's seat (leaning out the driver's side then sitting up), (b) keys were not in the ignition but produced from her person on request, and (c) the motor was not running. Note: a wrinkle the memo does not flag â€” the Barger court also relied on the headlights being on, which is a factor beyond "driver's seat + key in possession." That additional factor does not undermine the memo's narrower proposition, but Barger is not as cleanly limited to driver's-seat-plus-key as the memo's one-sentence framing implies. Still, the holding squarely supports the proposition. Supported. (The memo's [MEDIUM] confidence tag is conservative; Barger's holding on these facts is clear.)

proposition: Surveys the Kelley/Cleary/McGlone trilogy and observes that the Ohio Supreme Court had not expressly held that R.C. 4511.19 is violated where the intoxicated driver is behind the wheel and has possession of the ignition key but it is not in the ignition.
cited_case: State v. Jenkins, 75 Ohio App.3d 63 (4th Dist. 1991)
quoted_text: ["It does not appear that the Ohio Supreme Court has expressly held that R.C. 4511.19 is violated where the intoxicated driver is behind the wheel and has possession of the ignition key but it is not in the ignition."]
quote_check: [{"quote": "It does not appear that the Ohio Supreme Court has expressly held that R.C. 4511.19 is violated where the intoxicated driver is behind the wheel and has possession of the ignition key but it is not in the ignition.", "result": "VERBATIM", "similarity": 1.0, "matched_passage": "arising from the facts of the specific case before the court for adjudication.\u201d It does not appear that the Ohio Supreme Court has expressly held that R.C. 4511.19 is violated where the intoxicated driver is behind the wheel and has possession of the ignition key but it is not in the ignition. The Ohio Supreme held in Cincinnati v."}]
brief_block: State v. Jenkins, 75 Ohio App.3d 63, 70 (4th Dist. 1991), read the same way, then-uncertainly: "It does not appear that the Ohio Supreme Court has expressly held that R.C. 4511.19 is violated where the intoxicated driver is behind the wheel and has possession of the ignition key but it is not in the ignition." Jenkins surveyed the Kelley/Cleary/McGlone trilogy and concluded . . . that out-of-ignition possession sufficed.
opinion_block: It does not appear that the Ohio Supreme Court has expressly held that R.C. 4511.19 is violated where the intoxicated driver is behind the wheel and has possession of the ignition key but it is not in the ignition. The Ohio Supreme held in Cincinnati v. Kelly (1976), 47 Ohio St.2d 94, . . . that "[t]o be in actual physical control of an automobile . . . a person must be in the driver's seat of the vehicle, behind the steering wheel, in possession of the ignition key, and be in such condition that he is physically capable of starting the engine and causing the vehicle to move." [Jenkins at 70-71.]
finding_analysis: The verbatim quote is accurate against Jenkins at p. 70. The substantive observation (Ohio Supreme Court had not expressly held that out-of-ignition possession violates R.C. 4511.19) is directly supported. However, the memo's framing that Jenkins "surveys the Kelley/Cleary/McGlone trilogy" is overstated: Jenkins discusses Cleary at length and discusses Kelley once (in the very passage that contains the quoted sentence), but does not cite or discuss McGlone at all (a grep of the opinion for "McGlone" returns zero hits). Jenkins is therefore better described as surveying Kelley and Cleary, not the full "trilogy." The proposition also says Jenkins "concluded . . . that out-of-ignition possession sufficed" â€” the majority did affirm the conviction on a broad operation theory and reasoned that "the danger is as great to the public if the accused has possession of the key but has not placed it in the ignition," so this part is fairly characterized, though Jenkins reached that conclusion under Crim.R. 52(B) plain-error review (no objection had been preserved) and the court expressly noted the question was "open to question." Partial support overall: the verbatim observation is accurate, but the memo's characterization of Jenkins as surveying the McGlone case is inaccurate.

proposition: Outlier reversing OVI conviction where defendant was asleep in the driver's seat with the key in the car ashtray; lead opinion applied pre-2004 McGlone 'key in ignition' formulation literally. Effectively superseded by 2004 statute and by Gill.
cited_case: State v. Kincaid, 83 Ohio App.3d 341 (4th Dist. 1992)
brief_block: State v. Kincaid, 83 Ohio App.3d 341 (4th Dist. 1992), is the case at the outer edge: the defendant was asleep in the driver's seat with the key in the car ashtray. The lead opinion, applying the pre-2004 McGlone 'key in the ignition' formulation literally, reversed the conviction; the concurrence â€” Judge Grey â€” would have held the ashtray sufficient. Note that Kincaid construed OVI under the old framework that required the key to be 'in the ignition,' a doctrinal element the 2004 statute discarded for physical control. The result in Kincaid would almost certainly come out the other way today under R.C. 4511.194.
opinion_block: Here, the defendant was asleep, the keys were in the ashtray, the motor was not running, the car was legally parked, the defendant's hands were not on the steering wheel and there was no vehicular movement or operator-type conduct. There was no evidence of operation at all. . . . It would appear that where a person is asleep in a parked vehicle, having the key in the ignition is a bright line test for determining if that person is guilty of violating R.C. 4511.19(A)(1). . . . [I]f a person is drunk, asleep in a motor vehicle, with no key in the ignition, and there is no evidence of any conduct which might be included within the broad category of operation, he is not guilty of drunk driving. [Kincaid at 344-46.]
finding_analysis: No direct quotes â€” no quote check needed. The core factual and doctrinal characterization is supported: Kincaid was asleep slumped over in a parked car, the keys were in the ashtray rather than the ignition, and the Fourth District reversed an R.C. 4511.19(A)(1) conviction on the rationale that 'having the key in the ignition is a bright line test' for the sleeping-defendant scenario. The opinion expressly relies on the pre-2004 McGlone formulation (Kincaid block-quotes McGlone for the proposition that 'an intoxicated person who is in the driver's seat of a motor vehicle parked on private or public property with the key in the ignition is operating the vehicle in violation of R.C. 4511.19(A)(1).'). One small inaccuracy: the memo refers to 'the concurrence â€” Judge Grey â€” would have held the ashtray sufficient.' That gets the procedural posture wrong â€” Grey, J. is the AUTHOR of the lead/majority opinion, and Stephenson, P.J., concurs (in the result), with Abele, J., concurring separately. Grey's opinion is the one that reversed; there is no concurrence reading the ashtray as sufficient. The memo's framing in the proposition itself (that the 'lead opinion applied pre-2004 McGlone key-in-ignition formulation literally') is correct, and the misattribution of Grey to a non-existent concurrence is a stray internal inconsistency in the memo's narrative paragraph rather than a defect in the proposition. The 'effectively superseded by 2004 statute and by Gill' framing is reasonable commentary; no Ohio court has formally overruled Kincaid, but the doctrinal premise (operation requires key-in-ignition for sleeping defendants) is plainly inconsistent with post-2004 R.C. 4511.194 and with Gill's 'potential movement' rationale. Supported on the merits of the proposition, with a minor narrative slip on Judge Grey's procedural role.

proposition: Recent application of R.C. 4511.194 to a driver found unconscious in the driver's seat with keys in the vehicle, with no evidence the keys were in the ignition or the engine was running. Possession-within-the-vehicle treated as established.
syllabus: Appellant's conviction for operating a vehicle while intoxicated (OVI) was supported by legally sufficient evidence. However, the trial court erred in relying on an uncertified printout of a Law Enforcement Automatic Data System driving record as sufficient evidence to prove that appellant had at least five prior OVI convictions within a 20-year period. Judgment affirmed as modified remanded for resentencing.
quoted_text: ["no evidence that the keys were in the ignition or the engine was running"]
brief_block: In contrast, the most recent appellate snapshots â€” State v. Miller, 2024-Ohio-2009 (2d Dist.), and State v. Adams, 2024-Ohio-174 (2d Dist.) â€” apply R.C. 4511.194 routinely to drivers found unconscious in the driver's seat with keys in the vehicle (and, for Miller, 'no evidence that the keys were in the ignition or the engine was running'). Neither decision questions that keys-within-the-cabin satisfies possession. Miller affirmed the OVI conviction on circumstantial evidence of operation, but its discussion of the OVI/physical-control distinction treats possession-within-the-vehicle as established by the keys being inside the cabin.
opinion_block: Miller concedes that he was in physical control of the vehicle but argues that he was not operating the vehicle within the meaning of R.C. 4511.01(HHH). [Miller Â¶ 11.] . . . In this case, Miller's blood test following his arrest was positive for methamphetamine at a concentration exceeding the legal limit. The State set forth evidence that Miller was found slumped over the steering wheel in the driver's seat of a vehicle in a ditch on a rural road at around 2:00 a.m. Although there was no evidence that the keys were in the ignition or the engine was running, Deputy Toomey observed Miller alone in the vehicle, behind the steering wheel . . . . We agree with the trial court that these facts constituted circumstantial evidence that Miller had operated the vehicle to bring it to the side of the rural road where it was found. [Miller Â¶ 16.] . . . [T]he trial court . . . noted that the State had not presented direct evidence that Miller had possessed the vehicle's ignition key or other ignition device or that law enforcement officers had observed Miller driving the vehicle. [Miller Â¶ 7.]
finding_analysis: The quoted phrase 'no evidence that the keys were in the ignition or the engine was running' is verbatim against Miller Â¶ 16 (VERBATIM confirmed). However, the proposition substantially overstates what Miller holds about R.C. 4511.194 'possession of the ignition key.' Two problems: (1) Miller CONCEDED physical control on appeal. At Â¶ 11 the Second District states: 'Miller concedes that he was in physical control of the vehicle but argues that he was not operating the vehicle within the meaning of R.C. 4511.01(HHH).' The opinion's analysis is entirely about whether the State proved the additional element of OVI â€” 'operation,' i.e., movement of the vehicle â€” by circumstantial evidence. There is no litigated physical-control holding; the trial court merged the physical control count into the OVI count and the appeal challenged only OVI sufficiency. (2) The 'no evidence that the keys were in the ignition or the engine was running' language is doing OVI-sufficiency work, not physical-control work. The Second District uses the absence of evidence about ignition/engine to frame what the State had to make up with circumstantial evidence of prior movement; it is not saying that keys-not-in-ignition satisfies the 'possession of the ignition key' element of R.C. 4511.194. In fact, the opinion expressly notes (Â¶ 7) that the trial court itself observed 'that the State had not presented direct evidence that Miller had possessed the vehicle's ignition key or other ignition device.' The memo's characterization â€” that Miller treats 'possession-within-the-vehicle' as 'established' â€” has no support in the opinion's reasoning. It rests on the appellant's concession plus a sentence about absence of evidence pulled from the OVI sufficiency analysis. The memo's [HIGH] confidence tag is unwarranted; concession-by-the-appellant is not a litigated holding and cannot stand as appellate authority on the contours of R.C. 4511.194(A)(2). Partially supported: the factual setup (defendant unconscious behind the wheel, no evidence of key-in-ignition) and the verbatim quote are accurate, but the doctrinal characterization â€” that the Second District treats keys-within-the-cabin as satisfying R.C. 4511.194 possession â€” extends beyond what the opinion actually decides.

proposition: Recent application of R.C. 4511.194 to an impaired driver found unconscious in the driver's seat; possession-within-the-vehicle treated as established by the keys being inside the cabin.
syllabus: The trial court did not err in overruling appellant's motion to suppress. The law enforcement officers had reasonable, articulable suspicion that appellant was under the influence and, as he got out of his vehicle, an officer observed drugs in plain view inside the vehicle. At that point, the officers had probable cause to search the vehicle. Judgment affirmed.
brief_block: In contrast, the most recent appellate snapshots â€” State v. Miller, 2024-Ohio-2009 (2d Dist.), and State v. Adams, 2024-Ohio-174 (2d Dist.) â€” apply R.C. 4511.194 routinely to drivers found unconscious in the driver's seat with keys in the vehicle . . . . Neither decision questions that keys-within-the-cabin satisfies possession.
opinion_block: John Adams appeals from his conviction on one count of possession of drugs following a no contest plea; he argues that the trial court erred in overruling his motion to suppress. For the following reasons, we affirm the judgment of the trial court. [Adams Â¶ 1.] . . . The pickup truck was running and in drive, with Adams's foot on the brake enough to keep him from rolling forward. Medical personnel put the vehicle in park and removed the keys for safety. [Adams Â¶ 16.] . . . After the investigation, the officers issued Adams a citation for having physical control of the vehicle while under the influence. [Adams Â¶ 18.]
finding_analysis: Adams is NOT a R.C. 4511.194 case in any substantive sense, and the memo's characterization of it as a 'recent application' of the physical-control statute is incorrect. The metadata-check flag is confirmed: Adams's sole assignment of error is that 'the trial court erred in overruling his motion to suppress,' and the conviction on appeal is for POSSESSION OF HEROIN (Â¶ 1), not for R.C. 4511.194 physical control. The opinion's entire substantive analysis (Â¶Â¶ 7-23) is Fourth Amendment doctrine â€” community caretaking / emergency aid, Terry stop, plain view, automobile exception. R.C. 4511.194 appears in only two contexts: (a) the officers' subjective basis for continuing the encounter (suspicion that Adams had 'operated the vehicle while impaired'), and (b) the citation Adams was issued (Â¶ 18). The court does not interpret 'possession of the ignition key,' does not analyze whether Adams had 'physical control' as a doctrinal matter, and does not address the elements of R.C. 4511.194 at all. Beyond the legal-subject mismatch, the memo's factual framing of Adams is also wrong on the facts. The memo says Adams supports the proposition that 'possession-within-the-vehicle treated as established by the keys being inside the cabin.' But Adams's vehicle was RUNNING AND IN DRIVE with his foot on the brake (Â¶ 16); the keys were necessarily in the ignition. Medics had to put the truck in park and remove the keys for safety. So Adams is not even a 'keys-inside-the-cabin-but-not-in-ignition' case â€” it is a keys-in-ignition, vehicle-running case where the only litigated issue was a suppression motion on a separate drug charge. Adams cannot bear the weight the memo places on it. Not supported. This is a 'case on unrelated subject' problem: the Adams court did not address R.C. 4511.194 possession at all, and the factual hook the memo uses (keys-inside-the-cabin) is also factually inapposite. The proposition tagged this with [HIGH] confidence; that tag is unjustified.

proposition: General Ohio constructive-possession standard: constructive possession exists when an individual knowingly exercises dominion and control over an object even though that object may not be within his immediate physical possession.
quoted_text: ["[c]onstructive possession exists when an individual knowingly exercises dominion and control over an object, even though that object may not be within his immediate physical possession."]
quote_check: [{"quote": "[c]onstructive possession exists when an individual knowingly exercises dominion and control over an object, even though that object may not be within his immediate physical possession.", "result": "CLOSE", "similarity": 0.76, "matched_passage": "of stolen property is not a requisite of the offense of receiving stolen property, but that a conviction may be based on the accused\u2019s constructive possession of the property. Constructive possession exists when an individual exercises dominion and control over an object, even though that object may not be within his immediate physical possession. State v. Wolery, supra."}]
badge_label: Reworded â€” not a verbatim quote
brief_block: Ohio criminal practice generally recognizes both actual and constructive possession â€” i.e., that "[c]onstructive possession exists when an individual knowingly exercises dominion and control over an object, even though that object may not be within his immediate physical possession." State v. Hankerson, 70 Ohio St.2d 87, syllabus (1982). Whether the Hankerson concept of constructive possession imports cleanly into R.C. 4511.194 is unsettled at the appellate level . . . .
opinion_block: Constructive possession exists when an individual exercises dominion and control over an object, even though that object may not be within his immediate physical possession. State v. Wolery, supra. . . . [T]he mere fact that property is located within premises under one's control does not, of itself, constitute constructive possession. It must also be shown that the person was conscious of the presence of the object. [Hankerson at 91.]
finding_analysis: The proposition is substantively supported â€” Hankerson is correctly characterized as the leading Ohio Supreme Court articulation of constructive possession, and the memo appropriately flags (in the surrounding text and again in Part IV) that the import of this doctrine into R.C. 4511.194 is unsettled. But the quoted text is REWORDED, not verbatim. The opinion's actual sentence (at p. 91) reads: 'Constructive possession exists when an individual EXERCISES dominion and control over an object, even though that object may not be within his immediate physical possession.' The memo's quote inserts the word 'knowingly' between 'individual' and 'exercises' â€” 'Constructive possession exists when an individual KNOWINGLY exercises dominion and control over an object . . . .' This is a substantive insertion, not OCR noise. In Hankerson the conscious-awareness requirement is stated SEPARATELY in the next paragraph ('It must also be shown that the person was conscious of the presence of the object'); it is not part of the constructive-possession definition itself, which Hankerson lifts from State v. Wolery (1976). By splicing 'knowingly' into the definitional sentence, the memo collapses two distinct holdings into one and presents a paraphrase as a direct quote. Two additional notes: (1) The memo cites this to the 'syllabus' of Hankerson; the available opinion file does not include a separate syllabus block, and the quoted definition appears in the body of the Sweeney, J., majority opinion at page 91, not in any syllabus paragraph. The case's actual syllabus paragraphs (per Bluebook practice for older Ohio Supreme Court opinions) are about receiving stolen property and circumstantial-evidence sufficiency, not the abstract constructive-possession definition. (2) Hankerson was a RECEIVING-STOLEN-PROPERTY case (stolen stereo speakers found in the parents' home), not the 'drug-possession' framing that some constructive-possession discussions adopt â€” the memo does not assert that mischaracterization in this row, but readers should not infer that Hankerson is itself a drug case. Overall: the substantive proposition (general Ohio constructive-possession standard, not yet imported into R.C. 4511.194) is supported, but the quoted text is a paraphrase presented as a verbatim quote, so the row warrants the 'Reworded' yellow badge. The ~0.76 similarity flag from the metadata pass is accurate; this is not OCR noise but a real wording change.

proposition: Leading articulation of the totality-of-circumstances physical-control test, approving a non-exhaustive jury-instruction factor list including key location, vehicle state, driver position, awake/asleep, headlights, weather, etc. Touchstone is whether the defendant posed a present or imminent risk of putting the vehicle in motion.
quoted_text: ["whether the vehicle was running or the ignition was on; where the key was located; where and in what position the driver was found in the vehicle; whether the person was awake or asleep; if the vehicle's headlights were on; where the vehicle was stopped (in the road or legally parked); whether the driver had voluntarily pulled off the road; time of day and weather conditions; if the heater or air conditioner was on; whether the windows were up or down; and any explanation of the circumstances advanced by the defense.", "a present or imminent risk of putting the vehicle in motion"]
quote_check: [{"quote": "whether the vehicle was running or the ignition was on; where the key was located; where and in what position the driver was found in the vehicle; whether the person was awake or asleep; if the vehicle's headlights were on; where the vehicle was stopped (in the road or legally parked); whether the driver had voluntarily pulled off the road; time of day and weather conditions; if the heater or air conditioner was on; whether the windows were up or down; and any explanation of the circumstances advanced by the defense.", "result": "CLOSE", "similarity": 0.8, "matched_passage": "e trier of fact to consider the totality of the circumstances in determining whether defendant was in actual physical control of his vehicle.\u201d Id. at 326, 897 P.2d at 628 . The Court listed several factors to be considered in determining whether a defendant exercised actual physical control, including whether the vehicle was running or the ignition was on; where the key was located; where and in what position the driver was found in the vehicle; whether the person was awake or asleep; if the vehicle\u2019s headlights were on; where the vehicle was stopped (in the road or legally parked); whether the driver had voluntarily pulled off the road; time of day and weather conditions; if the heater or air conditioner was on; whether the windows were up or down; and any explanation of the circumstances advanced by the defense. Id. (citing Atkinson v. State, 331 Md. 199 , 627 A.2d 1019, 1027 (1993))."}, {"quote": "a present or imminent risk of putting the vehicle in motion", "result": "FABRICATED", "similarity": 0.6, "matched_passage": "n determining whether the defendant actually posed a threat to the public by the exercise of present or imminent control of the vehicle while impaired. This instruction captures Love\u2019s holding that \u201cactual physical control is a question for the fac"}]
brief_block: The leading articulation is the Arizona Supreme Court's decision in State v. Zaragoza, 221 Ariz. 49, 209 P.3d 629 (2009), which approved a non-exhaustive jury instruction listing factors including "whether the vehicle was running or the ignition was on; where the key was located; where and in what position the driver was found in the vehicle; whether the person was awake or asleep; if the vehicle's headlights were on; where the vehicle was stopped (in the road or legally parked); whether the driver had voluntarily pulled off the road; time of day and weather conditions; if the heater or air conditioner was on; whether the windows were up or down; and any explanation of the circumstances advanced by the defense." Id. at 53. The touchstone is whether the defendant posed "a present or imminent risk of putting the vehicle in motion."
opinion_block: The Court listed several factors to be considered in determining whether a defendant exercised actual physical control, including [the factor list quoted in the brief]. Id. (citing Atkinson v. State, 331 Md. 199, 627 A.2d 1019, 1027 (1993)). We further noted that this list is not exhaustive. [Zaragoza Â¶ 12, quoting State v. Love, 182 Ariz. 324, 326, 897 P.2d 626, 628 (1995).] . . . the instruction here closely tracks Love's conclusion that a person is in actual physical control when, under the totality of the facts, the person "posed a threat to the public by the exercise of present or imminent control" over a vehicle "while impaired." [Zaragoza Â¶ 17.] . . . It is up to you to examine all the available evidence and weigh its credibility in determining whether the defendant actually posed a threat to the public by the exercise of present or imminent control of the vehicle while impaired. [Zaragoza Â¶ 21, recommended jury instruction.] . . . whether the defendant's current or imminent control of the vehicle presented a real danger to [himself] [herself] or others at the time alleged. [Zaragoza Â¶ 21.]
finding_analysis:
The 11-factor list is verbatim against Zaragoza Â¶ 12 (with one trivial OCR variant), but a hidden citation point: Zaragoza did not author this list â€” it quotes Love (182 Ariz. 324 (1995)) verbatim, and Love in turn cited Atkinson v. State (Md. 1993). The memo treats the factor list as Zaragoza's articulation, when it is more accurately Zaragoza re-quoting Love.

The second quote â€” "a present or imminent risk of putting the vehicle in motion" â€” is FABRICATED. That exact phrase does not appear anywhere in Zaragoza. The court's actual touchstone language is materially different: (1) "present or imminent control" of the vehicle (Â¶Â¶ 17, 21), (2) "posed a threat to the public" / "presented a real danger to himself or others" (Â¶Â¶ 17, 21), and (3) "actually posed a threat to the public by the exercise of present or imminent control over [the vehicle] while impaired" (Â¶ 21). The memo's phrase appears to be a stitched paraphrase that substitutes "risk of putting the vehicle in motion" for the court's "control" / "threat to the public" / "real danger" language.

The substantive distinction matters. Zaragoza's touchstone is about present or imminent CONTROL â€” which the court treats as broader than imminent driving. The court explicitly rejected the lower court's proposal that the test should focus on the defendant's "ultimate purpose of placing the vehicle in motion" (Â¶ 20), holding that defendant's intent is not an element and that any instruction requiring the jury to consider the defendant's purpose "incorrectly states the law." The memo's paraphrase ("risk of putting the vehicle in motion") leans toward the very motion-focused framing Zaragoza rejected.

Support: the proposition that Zaragoza is a leading totality-of-circumstances articulation that approves a non-exhaustive multifactor jury instruction is fully supported. The proposition that the touchstone is "present or imminent risk of putting the vehicle in motion" is not â€” the actual touchstone is present or imminent CONTROL posing a threat or real danger to the public. Because the misstatement of the touchstone slightly extends Zaragoza toward a motion-focused test it explicitly declined to adopt, classification is Partially supported. With the FABRICATED quote, color is Yellow with badge "Paraphrase presented as direct quote".

proposition: Florida totality approach: defendant must be in or on the vehicle and have the capability to operate it, evaluated under the totality of circumstances.
quoted_text: ["physically in or on the vehicle", "the capability to operate"]
quote_check: [{"quote": "physically in or on the vehicle", "result": "VERBATIM", "similarity": 1.0, "matched_passage": "To prove this element, \u201cthe defendant must be physically in or on the vehicle and have the capability to operate the vehicle, regardless of whether [he][she]"}, {"quote": "the capability to operate", "result": "VERBATIM", "similarity": 1.0, "matched_passage": "ve the charge with evidence that the defendant was physically in a vehicle with the capability to operate it, regardless of whether she was actually operating it at the time."}]
brief_block: Florida is the other leading exemplar; its courts ask whether the defendant was "physically in or on the vehicle" and had "the capability to operate" it, evaluated under the totality of circumstances. Florida Std. Jury Instr. (Crim.) 28.1; State v. Fitzgerald, 63 So. 3d 75 (Fla. 2d DCA 2011).
opinion_block: To prove this element, "the defendant must be physically in or on the vehicle and have the capability to operate the vehicle, regardless of whether [he][she] is actually operating the vehicle at the time." Fla. Std. Jury Instr. (Crim.) 28.1. . . . In analyzing the necessary proof of this element, the First District reviewed cases from Alabama, where the courts review the totality of the circumstances to determine whether someone is in actual physical control. Jones v. State, 510 So.2d 1147, 1148 (Fla. 1st DCA 1987). [Fitzgerald, 63 So.3d at 77.]
finding_analysis:
Both quoted phrases are verbatim, but they are quoted from the Florida Standard Jury Instruction (Crim.) 28.1, not from Fitzgerald's own holding â€” Fitzgerald reproduces the jury-instruction language. The memo accurately credits the jury instruction in its parallel signal but presents Fitzgerald as the leading totality-of-circumstances case.

The substantive issue: Fitzgerald does NOT itself articulate or apply a "totality of the circumstances" framework. The phrase "totality of the circumstances" appears in Fitzgerald only once, and only to describe Alabama's approach (via Jones v. State citing Cagle v. City of Gadsden). Fitzgerald's own analysis is more straightforwardly a sufficiency-of-evidence ruling: defendant in driver's seat + keys readily produced from her hand + car stopped in intersection with lights on = prima facie case under the jury instruction's "physically in . . . and capability to operate" language. There is no enumerated factor list and no totality framework adopted as Florida's test in the opinion.

The memo's framing thus overstates Fitzgerald: it (a) attributes language to the case that the case itself attributes to the Florida Standard Jury Instruction, and (b) presents the case as embracing a "totality of circumstances" test when the case mentions that phrase only descriptively about Alabama law. The jury instruction quote and the basic proposition (Florida requires physical presence in the vehicle plus capability to operate) are supported; the totality-of-circumstances overlay is overstated relative to what Fitzgerald itself says. Partially supported.

proposition: Foundational Florida totality-of-circumstances articulation widely treated as the source of the camp-2 multifactor line.
brief_block: Fieselman v. State, 537 So. 2d 603 (Fla. 1988) [MEDIUM â€” verified via analyze_citations against CourtListener (cluster 1130447); full opinion not retrieved], is widely treated as the foundational Florida decision in the totality-of-circumstances line.
opinion_block: [Caption:] District Court of Appeal of Florida, Third District. November 22, 1988. . . . [Holding:] We believe that such an inference can be drawn since a person who has placed keys in the ignition of a vehicle may be as much in actual physical control of the vehicle as a person seated behind the wheel of the vehicle. . . . evidence that the key was in the ignition does not inexorably lead to the conclusion that the defendant was in actual physical control of the vehicle. It is merely a fact â€” along with the defendant's presence asleep and intoxicated in the vehicle â€” which, being capable of establishing the defendant's actual physical control of the vehicle, precludes the conclusion that as a matter of law the defendant was not in actual physical control of the vehicle and thus precludes the entry of a dismissal of the charges.
finding_analysis:
Two distinct problems.

First, the citation is wrong. The memo cites Fieselman as "537 So. 2d 603 (Fla. 1988)" â€” implying a Florida Supreme Court decision. Fieselman is in fact a Fla. 3d DCA decision (District Court of Appeal of Florida, Third District, November 22, 1988). The correct cite is "537 So. 2d 603 (Fla. 3d DCA 1988)." This matters because the memo's framing of Fieselman as "foundational" / leading authority would carry very different weight as a Florida Supreme Court decision vs. an intermediate appellate decision from one of five DCAs.

Second, and more importantly, Fieselman does not articulate a totality-of-circumstances test, does not enumerate any multifactor list, and is not in fact "the foundational Florida decision in the totality-of-circumstances line." Fieselman is a sufficiency / motion-to-dismiss ruling on a narrow question: whether the presence of keys in the ignition (combined with defendant lying down asleep in the front seat of a parked car in a parking lot, engine cold) sufficed to defeat a motion to dismiss. The opinion concludes that keys-in-ignition + presence-asleep-while-intoxicated is enough to take the case to the factfinder. Nothing in the opinion announces a totality test or a multifactor framework. The phrase "totality of the circumstances" does not appear in Fieselman. The opinion does not purport to set a general framework for Florida's physical-control jurisprudence; it is a case-specific certiorari denial.

The memo's parenthetical concedes the system did not retrieve the full opinion ("verified via analyze_citations against CourtListener (cluster 1130447); full opinion not retrieved"). On the actual opinion text, the proposition is not supported. Color is Red.

proposition: Maryland's leading articulation of the multifactor totality test; Zaragoza adopts its factor list verbatim.
brief_block: The Maryland Court of Appeals' decision in Atkinson v. State, 331 Md. 199, 627 A.2d 1019 (1993) [MEDIUM â€” verified against CourtListener (cluster 2337244); full opinion not retrieved; relied on as represented in Zaragoza's genealogy], is the most-cited articulation of the Zaragoza-style factor list and is treated by most commentators as the modern source of that approach rather than a Camp 4 outlier. Maryland's current articulation resembles the totality approach in Zaragoza.
opinion_block: [Atkinson factor list, 331 Md. at 216, 627 A.2d at 1027:] 1) whether or not the vehicle's engine is running, or the ignition on; 2) where and in what position the person is found in the vehicle; 3) whether the person is awake or asleep; 4) where the vehicle's ignition key is located; 5) whether the vehicle's headlights are on; 6) whether the vehicle is located in the roadway or is legally parked. . . . No one factor alone will necessarily be dispositive of whether the defendant was in "actual physical control" of the vehicle. Rather, each must be considered with an eye towards whether there is in fact present or imminent exercise of control over the vehicle or, instead, whether the vehicle is merely being used as a stationary shelter.
finding_analysis:
The first half of the proposition is supported: Atkinson IS Maryland's leading articulation of the multifactor totality test for "actual physical control" under Md. Transp. Â§ 21-902 / Â§ 11-114, and the Court of Appeals (then Maryland's highest court) enumerates a six-factor non-exhaustive list at 331 Md. at 216 and frames the inquiry around "present or imminent exercise of control" versus "stationary shelter." The memo's broader framing of Atkinson as the modern source of the camp-2 approach is defensible: Atkinson's framing is regularly cited by other state courts (including indirectly through Love in Zaragoza).

The second half â€” that "Zaragoza adopts [Atkinson's] factor list verbatim" â€” is not supported. Two distinct problems:

(1) Citation chain. Zaragoza did not adopt the list directly from Atkinson; Zaragoza quoted the list from its own prior decision in State v. Love, 182 Ariz. 324, 326 (1995). Love had earlier cited Atkinson when crafting its list. The memo's phrasing skips the Love step entirely.

(2) Not verbatim. Atkinson lists SIX factors: engine running/ignition on; position; awake/asleep; key location; headlights; roadway vs. legally parked. The list quoted in Zaragoza (from Love) contains ELEVEN-PLUS factors and adds several Atkinson does not have: whether the driver had voluntarily pulled off the road; time of day; weather conditions; whether the heater or air conditioner was on; whether the windows were up or down; and any explanation advanced by the defense. Love expanded Atkinson's list substantially. Even the overlapping items are rephrased (e.g., Atkinson's "where the vehicle is located in the roadway or is legally parked" appears in Love/Zaragoza as "where the vehicle was stopped (in the road or legally parked); whether the driver had voluntarily pulled off the road" â€” two factors, not one). "Adopts . . . verbatim" is therefore inaccurate; the more accurate framing would be "derives from and expands" or "builds on."

Because the genealogy claim is overstated and the "verbatim" claim is specifically false, classification is Partially supported. The core characterization of Atkinson as Maryland's leading totality articulation holds; the lineage detail does not.

proposition: Unpublished pre-May-1-2002 decision quoted approvingly in Zingale. Not officially controlling under Ohio Rule for Reporting Opinions 3.4 (effective May 1 2002); cited as persuasive only.
cited_case: Broadview Heights v. Soukup, Cuyahoga App. No. 64918 (Mar. 10, 1994)
diagnostics: Insufficient data to verify: citation text is missing both court and date. A match cannot be confirmed with name alone. Try adding the court and year parenthetical (e.g. '(E.D. Tenn. 2020)') to the citation text.
quoted_text: ["the ignition key of his vehicle need only be in close proximity to a defendant to constitute sufficient evidence defendant was operating his vehicle within the meaning of state DUI laws."]
brief_block: Unpublished pre-May-1-2002 decision quoted only through Zingale. The memo flags it as not officially controlling under Ohio Rule for Reporting Opinions 3.4 and treats it as persuasive only.
finding_analysis: Broadview Heights v. Soukup is not available on CourtListener as an independent cluster. The opinion text cannot be retrieved, so its existence and the quoted close-proximity language cannot be independently verified through CL. The memo itself acknowledges this status (unpublished pre-2002, persuasive only). Routing to unable-to-verify.
